topics.meaning: Integers are the collection of whole numbers and their negatives, shown on a number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Integers are the set of numbers that includes all the whole numbers (0, 1, 2, 3, ...) together with the negative numbers (-1, -2, -3, ...). So integers are ..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, .... We can show integers on a number line, with zero in the middle, positive integers to the right and negative integers to the left. On the number line, a number is greater if it lies to the right, so 3 is greater than -5. Integers are used in daily life to show things like temperatures below zero, money owed, and depths below sea level.
topics.easyExplanation: Integers are whole numbers plus their negatives: ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, .... On a number line, numbers to the right are greater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What integers are
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Integers include all whole numbers and their negatives.

stepByStepExplanation.title: On a number line
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Integers are shown on a number line with 0 in the middle.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Positives and negatives
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Positive integers are to the right of 0, negative integers to the left.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Comparing integers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A number to the right is greater, so 3 > -5.

examples: -3, 0 and 5 are all integers.
On the number line, -5 lies to the left of -1, so -5 is smaller.

importantKeywords.definition: A whole number or its negative.
A line showing numbers in order.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Integers are marked on a number line.

importantKeywords.definition: A number less than zero.

topics.quickSummary: Integers are the whole numbers together with their negatives, shown on a number line with positives to the right of zero and negatives to the left; a number further right is greater.
revisionNotes: Shown on a number line, 0 in the middle.
Right is greater; left is smaller.

topics.meaning: Integers are added and subtracted using rules of signs, often pictured on a number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To add and subtract integers we use rules about their signs. To add two integers with the same sign, we add their values and keep the sign (for example, -3 + -4 = -7). To add two integers with different signs, we subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the sign of the larger (for example, -7 + 5 = -2). To subtract an integer, we add its opposite (additive inverse); for example, 5 - (-3) = 5 + 3 = 8. Addition of integers is commutative and associative, and zero is the additive identity. These rules let us calculate confidently with positive and negative numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: To add integers: same signs add and keep the sign; different signs subtract and take the larger's sign. To subtract, add the opposite.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Same signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Add the values and keep the common sign, like -3 + -4 = -7.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Different signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Subtract the smaller value from the larger and take the larger's sign, like -7 + 5 = -2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Subtracting
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: To subtract an integer, add its opposite, like 5 - (-3) = 8.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Properties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Addition is commutative and associative; zero is the additive identity.

importantKeywords.definition: The opposite of a number, which adds to give zero.
Order does not change the result.
The number (0) that does not change a sum.

topics.quickSummary: Integers are added by adding same-sign values (keeping the sign) or subtracting different-sign values (taking the larger's sign), and subtracted by adding the opposite; addition is commutative and associative.
revisionNotes: Same signs: add, keep the sign.
Different signs: subtract, take larger's sign.
Addition is commutative and associative; 0 is identity.

topics.meaning: When multiplying integers, two like signs give a positive product and two unlike signs give a negative product.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we multiply integers, the sign of the answer depends on the signs of the numbers. The product of two positive integers is positive, and the product of two negative integers is also positive (like signs give a positive). The product of a positive and a negative integer is negative (unlike signs give a negative). For example, (-4) x (-3) = 12, but (-4) x 3 = -12. To find the product, we multiply the values and then apply the sign rule. Multiplication of integers is commutative, associative, and 1 is the multiplicative identity; multiplying by 0 gives 0.
topics.easyExplanation: Multiplying integers: like signs (++ or --) give a positive answer; unlike signs (+ and -) give a negative answer. So (-4) x (-3) = 12.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The product of two positives or two negatives is positive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The product of a positive and a negative is negative.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the product
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the values, then apply the sign rule.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Properties
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiplication is commutative, associative; 1 is the identity and multiplying by 0 gives 0.

importantKeywords.definition: The result of multiplying.
Signs that are the same.
The number (1) that does not change a product.

topics.quickSummary: When multiplying integers, like signs give a positive product and unlike signs give a negative product; multiplication is commutative, associative, with 1 as identity and 0 giving 0.

topics.meaning: When dividing integers, like signs give a positive quotient and unlike signs give a negative quotient.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Division of integers follows the same sign rules as multiplication. When we divide two integers with like signs (both positive or both negative), the quotient is positive; when we divide two integers with unlike signs, the quotient is negative. For example, (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4, but (-12) ÷ 3 = -4. To divide, we divide the values and apply the sign rule. Note that we cannot divide any number by zero. Understanding division of integers lets us solve a wider range of problems involving positive and negative numbers.
topics.easyExplanation: Dividing integers: like signs give a positive answer; unlike signs give a negative answer. So (-12) ÷ (-3) = 4. We cannot divide by zero.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Like signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dividing two integers with the same sign gives a positive quotient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Unlike signs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dividing two integers with different signs gives a negative quotient.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the quotient
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the values, then apply the sign rule.

stepByStepExplanation.title: No division by zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: We cannot divide any number by zero.

importantKeywords.definition: The result of dividing.
Splitting into equal parts.
Signs that are different.

topics.meaning: Fractions are multiplied by multiplying the numerators together and the denominators together.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply two fractions, we multiply the numerators (top numbers) together to get the new numerator, and multiply the denominators (bottom numbers) together to get the new denominator, then simplify. For example, (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. To multiply a fraction by a whole number, we multiply the whole number with the numerator. The word 'of' in problems means multiplication, so '1/2 of 10' means (1/2) x 10 = 5. When we multiply a proper fraction by another proper fraction, the product is smaller than both, which is a special feature of fractions less than 1.
topics.easyExplanation: To multiply fractions, multiply the tops together and the bottoms together, then simplify. So (2/3) x (4/5) = 8/15. 'Of' means multiply.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply numerators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the top numbers to get the new numerator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply denominators
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the bottom numbers to get the new denominator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Reduce the answer to its simplest form, like 8/15.

importantKeywords.definition: The top number of a fraction.

topics.meaning: To divide by a fraction, we multiply by its reciprocal (the fraction turned upside down).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Dividing by a fraction is done by multiplying by its reciprocal. The reciprocal of a fraction is found by interchanging its numerator and denominator; for example, the reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3. To divide one fraction by another, we keep the first fraction, change the division sign to multiplication, and use the reciprocal of the second fraction. For example, (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 10/12 = 5/6. The same method works to divide a whole number by a fraction or a fraction by a whole number. This makes division of fractions simple once we know the reciprocal.
topics.easyExplanation: To divide by a fraction, flip it (its reciprocal) and multiply. So (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find the reciprocal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The reciprocal of a fraction interchanges its numerator and denominator, like 3/4 to 4/3.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Change to multiplication
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Keep the first fraction and change ÷ to x.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply by the reciprocal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply by the reciprocal of the second fraction.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Simplify
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Simplify the answer, like (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = 5/6.

examples: The reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3.

importantKeywords.definition: A fraction turned upside down.
Splitting into equal parts.

revisionNotes: Divide by a fraction = multiply by its reciprocal.

topics.meaning: Decimals are multiplied like whole numbers, then the decimal point is placed by counting decimal places.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To multiply decimal numbers, we first multiply them as if they were whole numbers, ignoring the decimal points. Then we count the total number of digits after the decimal points in both numbers, and place the decimal point in the answer so that it has that many decimal places. For example, 2.5 x 1.2: multiplying 25 x 12 = 300, and there are two decimal places in total, so the answer is 3.00 = 3. To multiply a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we simply move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right. These rules make decimal multiplication straightforward.
topics.easyExplanation: Multiply decimals like whole numbers, then count the total decimal places and place the point. Multiplying by 10, 100, 1000 moves the point right.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiply as whole numbers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ignore the decimal points and multiply as whole numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Count the total digits after the decimal points in both numbers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Place the point
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put the decimal point in the answer with that many decimal places.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move the decimal point one, two or three places to the right.

topics.meaning: Decimals are divided by adjusting the decimal point, especially by making the divisor a whole number.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To divide decimals, we handle the decimal point carefully. To divide a decimal by 10, 100 or 1000, we move the decimal point one, two or three places to the left; for example, 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45. To divide a decimal by a whole number, we divide as usual and place the decimal point in the quotient directly above its place in the dividend. To divide a decimal by another decimal, we first make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both numbers by 10, 100 or 1000, and then divide. These methods let us divide decimal numbers accurately.
topics.easyExplanation: To divide decimals: by 10, 100, 1000 move the point left; by a whole number, divide normally; by a decimal, make the divisor a whole number first.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By 10, 100, 1000
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move the decimal point one, two or three places to the left, like 4.5 ÷ 10 = 0.45.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By a whole number
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide as usual and place the point above its place in the dividend.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By a decimal
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Make the divisor a whole number by multiplying both by 10, 100 or 1000.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Then divide
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Divide the adjusted numbers to get the answer.

examples: 2.5 ÷ 0.5 = 25 ÷ 5 = 5 (after multiplying both by 10).

topics.meaning: Data is information collected for a purpose, and it is organised in tables to study it easily.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Data is a collection of numbers or information gathered for a particular purpose, such as the marks of students or the runs scored in matches. Raw data collected as it comes is hard to read, so we organise it. We can arrange data in order, and use a frequency distribution table with tally marks to show how many times each value occurs (its frequency). The smallest and largest values give us the range of the data. Organising data well is the first step to understanding it and drawing useful conclusions.
topics.easyExplanation: Data is collected information, like students' marks. We organise it in tables with tally marks and frequencies so it is easy to read and study.

topics.meaning: Mean, median and mode are three measures that represent the central or typical value of data.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To describe a set of data with a single representative value, we use measures of central tendency. The mean (arithmetic average) is found by adding all the values and dividing by the number of values. The median is the middle value when the data is arranged in order; if there are two middle values, the median is their average. The mode is the value that occurs most often in the data. Each measure tells us something about the 'typical' value, and which one to use depends on the situation. These measures help us summarise and compare data.
topics.easyExplanation: Mean is the average (sum ÷ count), median is the middle value in order, and mode is the most frequent value. They show the typical value of data.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mean
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The average: add all values and divide by the number of values.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Median

stepByStepExplanation.title: Mode

stepByStepExplanation.title: Their use
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Each gives a typical value; which to use depends on the situation.

examples: For 2, 4, 6, the mean is (2+4+6)/3 = 4.
For 3, 5, 7, 9, the median is (5+7)/2 = 6; for data 2, 3, 3, 5, the mode is 3.

topics.meaning: A bar graph shows data using bars, making it easy to compare quantities at a glance.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A bar graph represents data using rectangular bars of equal width, where the length or height of each bar shows the value it represents. The bars are drawn with equal gaps on a graph with two axes and a suitable scale. Bar graphs make it easy to compare different quantities just by looking at the bars. A double bar graph uses two bars side by side for each item, which is useful for comparing two sets of data at once, such as the marks of two students in different subjects. Bar graphs present data clearly and are widely used.
topics.easyExplanation: A bar graph shows data with bars, so longer bars mean larger values. A double bar graph compares two sets of data side by side.

topics.meaning: Probability tells us how likely an event is to happen, on a scale from impossible to certain.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: In daily life some events are certain, some are impossible, and many are uncertain. Probability is a way of measuring how likely an uncertain event is to happen. For an experiment with equally likely outcomes, the probability of an event is the number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. For example, when tossing a coin, there are two outcomes (head or tail), so the probability of getting a head is 1/2. Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Understanding chance helps us reason about games, weather and many real-life situations.
topics.easyExplanation: Probability measures how likely an event is, from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). Tossing a coin, the chance of a head is 1/2.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Events differ in chance
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some events are certain, some impossible, and many uncertain.

stepByStepExplanation.title: What probability is
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Probability measures how likely an uncertain event is to happen.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate it
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Probability = favourable outcomes / total outcomes for equally likely outcomes.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

examples: The probability of getting a head when tossing a coin is 1/2.

topics.meaning: An equation is a statement that two expressions are equal, often containing an unknown called a variable.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An equation is a mathematical statement that says two expressions are equal, shown by an equals sign. It usually contains a variable, an unknown quantity represented by a letter like x. For example, x + 3 = 8 is an equation. We can set up an equation from a word problem by representing the unknown with a variable and writing the given condition as an equation. The value of the variable that makes the two sides equal is called the solution of the equation. Equations are powerful tools for solving real-life problems where some quantity is unknown.
topics.easyExplanation: An equation says two expressions are equal, like x + 3 = 8, with a variable (unknown) such as x. We can write equations from word problems.

topics.meaning: An equation is solved by doing the same operation on both sides to find the value of the variable.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To solve an equation means to find the value of the variable that makes it true. We do this by keeping the equation balanced: whatever operation we do to one side, we must do the same to the other side. We can add, subtract, multiply or divide both sides by the same number to gradually get the variable alone on one side. For example, to solve x + 3 = 8, we subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5. Another method is transposing, where we move a term to the other side and change its sign. We can check the solution by putting it back into the equation.
topics.easyExplanation: To solve an equation, do the same operation to both sides to get the variable alone. For x + 3 = 8, subtract 3 from both sides to get x = 5.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Isolate the variable

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Put the value back into the equation to check it is correct.

examples: x + 3 = 8 gives x = 5 by subtracting 3 from both sides.
2x = 10 gives x = 5 by dividing both sides by 2.

topics.meaning: Simple equations are used to solve real-life word problems involving an unknown quantity.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Simple equations are very useful for solving everyday problems where some quantity is unknown. To solve a word problem, we read it carefully, choose a variable for the unknown, write an equation from the given conditions, solve the equation, and then state the answer in words. For example, 'Three times a number plus 4 is 19' becomes 3x + 4 = 19, which gives x = 5. We should always check that our answer fits the original problem. This shows how algebra helps us turn real situations into equations and find the unknown.

topics.meaning: Pairs of angles can be related as complementary, supplementary, adjacent, linear pair or vertically opposite.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Some pairs of angles have special relationships. Two angles are complementary if their measures add up to 90 degrees, and supplementary if they add up to 180 degrees. Two angles are adjacent if they have a common vertex and a common arm but do not overlap. A linear pair is a pair of adjacent angles whose non-common arms form a straight line, so they add up to 180 degrees. Vertically opposite angles are formed when two lines cross, and they are always equal. Knowing these relationships helps us find unknown angles.
topics.easyExplanation: Angles can be complementary (add to 90), supplementary (add to 180), adjacent, a linear pair (add to 180), or vertically opposite (equal).

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Angles opposite each other when two lines cross are always equal.

topics.meaning: Lines can be intersecting or parallel, and a transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we study lines, we look at how they relate to each other. Two lines that cross each other at a point are called intersecting lines, and the point where they meet is the point of intersection. Two lines in the same plane that never meet, no matter how far they are extended, are called parallel lines. A transversal is a line that crosses two or more lines at different points. When a transversal cuts two lines, it forms several pairs of angles, such as corresponding angles, alternate angles and co-interior angles, which are important for studying parallel lines.
topics.easyExplanation: Lines that cross are intersecting; lines that never meet are parallel. A transversal is a line that cuts two or more lines, forming pairs of angles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Intersecting lines

topics.topic: Angles made by a Transversal on Parallel Lines
topics.meaning: When a transversal cuts parallel lines, corresponding and alternate angles are equal, and co-interior angles are supplementary.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When a transversal crosses a pair of parallel lines, special angle relationships appear. Corresponding angles (in matching positions) are equal. Alternate interior angles (on opposite sides of the transversal between the lines) are equal, and alternate exterior angles are also equal. Co-interior angles (on the same side of the transversal between the lines) are supplementary, adding up to 180 degrees. These properties let us find unknown angles when parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and they can also be used to check whether two lines are parallel.
topics.easyExplanation: When a transversal cuts parallel lines: corresponding angles are equal, alternate angles are equal, and co-interior angles add to 180 degrees.

topics.topic: Medians and Altitudes of a Triangle
topics.meaning: A median joins a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side, and an altitude is the perpendicular from a vertex to the opposite side.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A triangle has special line segments. A median of a triangle is a line segment that joins a vertex (corner) to the midpoint of the opposite side. Every triangle has three medians, and they all meet at a single point. An altitude of a triangle is the perpendicular line segment drawn from a vertex to the opposite side (or the line containing it). Every triangle has three altitudes. The altitude represents the 'height' of the triangle from a particular side. Knowing medians and altitudes helps us understand the structure of triangles and is useful in finding areas.

topics.topic: Angle Sum Property and Exterior Angle
topics.meaning: The three angles of a triangle add up to 180 degrees, and an exterior angle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two important properties relate to the angles of a triangle. The angle sum property states that the sum of the three interior angles of any triangle is always 180 degrees. So if two angles are known, the third can be found by subtracting their sum from 180 degrees. The exterior angle property states that an exterior angle of a triangle (formed by extending one side) is equal to the sum of the two interior opposite angles. These properties are very useful for finding unknown angles in triangles and in many geometry problems.

topics.topic: Types of Triangles and the Triangle Inequality
topics.meaning: Triangles are classified by their sides and angles, and the sum of any two sides is always greater than the third.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Triangles can be classified in two ways. By their sides: an equilateral triangle has all three sides equal, an isosceles triangle has two sides equal, and a scalene triangle has all sides different. By their angles: an acute triangle has all angles less than 90 degrees, a right triangle has one angle of 90 degrees, and an obtuse triangle has one angle more than 90 degrees. An important rule is the triangle inequality: the sum of the lengths of any two sides of a triangle is always greater than the length of the third side. This rule tells us whether three given lengths can form a triangle.
topics.easyExplanation: Triangles are classified by sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) and angles (acute, right, obtuse). Any two sides always add to more than the third.

stepByStepExplanation.title: By angles

stepByStepExplanation.title: Triangle inequality
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The sum of any two sides is always greater than the third side.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Forming a triangle

topics.meaning: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The Pythagoras theorem is an important property of right-angled triangles (triangles with one 90-degree angle). The side opposite the right angle is the longest side, called the hypotenuse. The theorem states that the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. If the two shorter sides are a and b and the hypotenuse is c, then a-squared + b-squared = c-squared. For example, in a triangle with sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5 because 9 + 16 = 25. This theorem is used to find an unknown side and to check whether a triangle is right-angled.
topics.easyExplanation: In a right triangle, (hypotenuse)^2 = (side1)^2 + (side2)^2. For sides 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is 5, since 9 + 16 = 25.

topics.meaning: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Two figures are said to be congruent if they are exactly the same in both shape and size, so that one can be placed exactly over the other and they cover each other completely. For example, two line segments are congruent if they have the same length, and two angles are congruent if they have the same measure. Congruent figures are like exact copies of each other. The symbol for congruence is a special equals sign with a curve on top. When two triangles are congruent, their corresponding sides and corresponding angles are equal. Understanding congruence helps us compare shapes precisely.
topics.easyExplanation: Two figures are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size, so one fits exactly over the other. Congruent triangles have equal sides and angles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Examples
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Segments are congruent if equal in length; angles if equal in measure.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Congruent triangles
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Their corresponding sides and angles are equal.

topics.meaning: Triangles can be shown to be congruent using the SSS, SAS, ASA and RHS criteria.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We do not need to check all six measurements (three sides and three angles) to prove two triangles are congruent; certain sets are enough. The SSS (Side-Side-Side) criterion: if the three sides of one triangle equal the three sides of another, they are congruent. The SAS (Side-Angle-Side) criterion: if two sides and the angle between them are equal, the triangles are congruent. The ASA (Angle-Side-Angle) criterion: if two angles and the side between them are equal, they are congruent. The RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) criterion applies to right triangles: if the hypotenuse and one side are equal, they are congruent. Note that AAA (all angles equal) does not prove congruence, only similar shape.
topics.easyExplanation: Triangles are congruent if they match by SSS (three sides), SAS (two sides and included angle), ASA (two angles and included side), or RHS (right triangles).

stepByStepExplanation.title: SSS
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Three sides of one triangle equal to three sides of another make them congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: ASA
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Two angles and the side between them equal make triangles congruent.

stepByStepExplanation.title: RHS
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For right triangles, equal hypotenuse and one side make them congruent (AAA does not work).

examples: Two triangles with all three sides equal are congruent by SSS.
Two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one side are congruent by RHS.

topics.meaning: A ratio compares two quantities, a proportion equates two ratios, and a percentage is a fraction out of 100.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We often compare quantities. A ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written as a : b. When two ratios are equal, they are said to be in proportion. A percentage is a special way of comparing a quantity to 100; 'per cent' means 'out of 100', written with the symbol %. We can convert between fractions, decimals and percentages: for example, 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%. To find a percentage of a quantity, we multiply; for example, 20% of 50 = (20/100) x 50 = 10. Percentages are widely used in marks, discounts and comparisons.
topics.easyExplanation: A ratio compares two quantities (a : b); equal ratios are in proportion; a percentage is 'out of 100'. So 1/2 = 50%, and 20% of 50 = 10.

topics.meaning: Profit or loss is found by comparing the cost price and selling price of an item, often as a percentage.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When something is bought and sold, we compare its cost price (CP), the price at which it is bought, with its selling price (SP), the price at which it is sold. If the selling price is more than the cost price, there is a profit (gain); if it is less, there is a loss. Profit = SP - CP, and Loss = CP - SP. Profit or loss is often expressed as a percentage of the cost price: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100, and Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100. These ideas are used in everyday buying and selling to know how much was gained or lost.
topics.easyExplanation: Profit happens when selling price is more than cost price; loss when it is less. Profit% and Loss% are found as a percentage of the cost price.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Calculate them
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Profit = SP - CP; Loss = CP - SP.

stepByStepExplanation.title: As a percentage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Profit% = (Profit / CP) x 100; Loss% = (Loss / CP) x 100.

If CP = 80 and profit = 20, then Profit% = (20/80) x 100 = 25%.

topics.meaning: Simple interest is the extra money paid for borrowing or earned for lending, calculated on the principal.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: When we borrow money or deposit money in a bank, we pay or earn extra money called interest. The money borrowed or deposited is called the principal (P). The interest is usually charged at a certain rate per cent per year, called the rate (R), for a certain time in years (T). Simple interest is calculated using the formula: SI = (P x R x T) / 100. The total amount to be paid back is the Amount = Principal + Simple Interest. For example, the simple interest on 1000 rupees at 10% for 2 years is (1000 x 10 x 2)/100 = 200 rupees. Simple interest is used in everyday money matters.
topics.easyExplanation: Simple interest is the extra money on a loan or deposit: SI = (P x R x T)/100. The total to repay is Amount = Principal + Interest.

topics.meaning: A rational number is a number that can be written as a fraction p/q, where q is not zero.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A rational number is any number that can be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q is not zero. This includes all integers, fractions and their negatives; for example, 3, -2, 1/2 and -3/4 are all rational numbers. A rational number is positive if its numerator and denominator have the same sign, and negative if they have different signs. We can show rational numbers on a number line, just like integers and fractions. A rational number is in its standard form when the denominator is positive and the numerator and denominator have no common factor other than 1.
topics.easyExplanation: A rational number can be written as p/q (q not zero), like 1/2, -3/4 or 3. They can be positive or negative and shown on a number line.

topics.meaning: Rational numbers are compared by writing them with the same denominator or by using the number line.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: To compare two rational numbers and find which is greater, we can write them with the same positive denominator and then compare the numerators; the one with the larger numerator is greater. We can also compare them using a number line, where a number to the right is greater. Every positive rational number is greater than zero, and every negative rational number is less than zero, so positive numbers are always greater than negative ones. There are also infinitely many rational numbers between any two given rational numbers. Comparing rational numbers lets us order them.
topics.easyExplanation: To compare rational numbers, give them the same denominator and compare numerators, or use a number line. Positive numbers are always greater than negatives.

topics.meaning: Rational numbers can be added, subtracted, multiplied and divided using rules like those for fractions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can do all four operations with rational numbers. To add or subtract rational numbers, we write them with the same denominator and then add or subtract the numerators. To multiply two rational numbers, we multiply the numerators together and the denominators together. To divide by a rational number, we multiply by its reciprocal. The sign rules for integers also apply. For example, (2/3) + (1/3) = 1, (2/3) x (3/4) = 1/2, and (2/3) ÷ (4/5) = (2/3) x (5/4) = 5/6. These operations let us calculate with all rational numbers, positive and negative.
topics.easyExplanation: Add/subtract rational numbers with a common denominator; multiply by multiplying tops and bottoms; divide by multiplying by the reciprocal. Sign rules apply.

topics.meaning: Using a ruler and compass, we can construct a line parallel to a given line through a point not on it.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Practical geometry is about drawing accurate figures using a ruler and a compass. One important construction is drawing a line parallel to a given line through a point outside it. The method uses the property that when a transversal cuts two lines making equal alternate (or corresponding) angles, the lines are parallel. We draw a transversal from the point to the given line, copy the angle at the new point using a compass, and draw the parallel line. This construction relies on the angle properties of parallel lines and gives us an exact parallel line without measuring with a protractor.

topics.meaning: Triangles can be constructed accurately when certain measurements are given, using SSS, SAS, ASA or RHS conditions.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can construct a triangle accurately using a ruler, compass and protractor when enough measurements are given. The conditions match the congruence criteria. With SSS, we are given the three sides. With SAS, we are given two sides and the angle between them. With ASA, we are given two angles and the side between them. With RHS, we construct a right triangle given the hypotenuse and one side. In each case, we draw the parts in order, using the compass to mark lengths and the protractor for angles, to complete the triangle. These constructions show how the congruence conditions also tell us when a triangle is uniquely determined.

topics.topic: Perimeter and Area of Squares and Rectangles
topics.meaning: Perimeter is the distance around a shape and area is the surface it covers; both have formulas for squares and rectangles.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The perimeter of a closed figure is the total distance around its boundary, and the area is the amount of surface it covers. For a rectangle with length l and breadth b, the perimeter is 2 x (l + b) and the area is l x b. For a square with side s, the perimeter is 4 x s and the area is s x s. Perimeter is measured in units of length (like cm) and area in square units (like square cm). These formulas help us find the boundary length and surface of common shapes, useful for fencing, flooring and many real tasks.
topics.easyExplanation: Perimeter is the distance around a shape; area is the surface it covers. Rectangle: perimeter 2(l+b), area l x b. Square: perimeter 4s, area s x s.

topics.meaning: The area of a parallelogram is base times height, and the area of a triangle is half of base times height.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can find the areas of triangles and parallelograms using simple formulas. The area of a parallelogram is equal to its base multiplied by its height (the perpendicular distance between the base and the opposite side): Area = base x height. A triangle is half of a parallelogram, so the area of a triangle is half of base times height: Area = (1/2) x base x height. In both, the height must be measured perpendicular to the base. These formulas let us find the areas of many shapes, since complex shapes can be split into triangles and parallelograms.

topics.topic: Circumference and Area of a Circle
topics.meaning: The circumference of a circle is the distance around it, and both circumference and area use the number pi.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A circle is a round closed figure, and the distance around it is called its circumference. The distance from the centre to any point on the circle is the radius (r), and the distance across through the centre is the diameter (d = 2r). The circumference of a circle is given by C = 2 x pi x r (or pi x d), where pi is a special number approximately equal to 22/7 or 3.14. The area of a circle is given by A = pi x r x r (pi times the radius squared). These formulas let us find the boundary and surface of circular objects, like wheels, plates and pipes.
topics.easyExplanation: A circle's circumference (distance around) is 2 x pi x r, and its area is pi x r x r, where pi is about 22/7 or 3.14, and r is the radius.

topics.meaning: Algebraic expressions are made of terms, which are formed from factors, and the number part of a term is its coefficient.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression is formed using variables (letters) and constants (numbers) joined by operations. An expression is made up of terms, which are separated by plus or minus signs; for example, in 3x + 5, the terms are 3x and 5. Each term is a product of factors; in the term 3x, the factors are 3 and x. The numerical factor of a term is called its coefficient; in 3x, the coefficient of x is 3. Terms that have the same variables (like 3x and 5x) are called like terms, while terms with different variables are unlike terms. Understanding these parts is the basis of working with algebra.
topics.easyExplanation: Algebraic expressions are made of terms (like 3x and 5), each made of factors. The number in a term is its coefficient. Like terms have the same variables.

topics.topic: Types of Expressions and Their Addition
topics.meaning: Expressions are named by the number of terms, and like terms can be added or subtracted.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Algebraic expressions are named by how many terms they have: a monomial has one term (like 3x), a binomial has two terms (like 3x + 5), and a trinomial has three terms (like x squared + 2x + 1); expressions with one or more terms in general are called polynomials. We can add and subtract expressions by combining their like terms: we add or subtract the coefficients of like terms and keep the variable part the same. For example, 3x + 5x = 8x, and (2x + 3) + (4x + 1) = 6x + 4. Unlike terms cannot be combined. This lets us simplify and work with expressions.
topics.easyExplanation: Expressions are monomials (1 term), binomials (2 terms) or trinomials (3 terms). We add expressions by combining like terms, so 3x + 5x = 8x.

topics.topic: Finding the Value of an Expression
topics.meaning: The value of an algebraic expression is found by substituting given numbers for the variables.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: An algebraic expression has different values depending on the value of its variable. To find the value of an expression, we substitute (put in) the given number in place of the variable and then calculate. For example, the value of 3x + 5 when x = 2 is 3 x 2 + 5 = 11. This idea is used to make and use formulas, such as the formula for the perimeter of a square (4 x side) or the area of a rectangle (length x breadth), where we substitute the given measurements. Finding the value of expressions connects algebra to real calculations.

topics.meaning: An exponent shows how many times a number (the base) is multiplied by itself.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Exponents give us a short way to write repeated multiplication. When a number is multiplied by itself several times, we write it using a base and an exponent (or power); for example, 2 x 2 x 2 = 2 cubed, written as 2 with a small 3, which equals 8. Here 2 is the base and 3 is the exponent, meaning 2 is used three times. Exponents help us write very large numbers in a short form. For example, 10 raised to the power 5 means 10 multiplied by itself five times, which is 1,00,000. Reading and writing numbers with exponents makes large calculations much easier.
topics.easyExplanation: An exponent shows repeated multiplication: 2 cubed (2 to the power 3) means 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. The base is 2 and the exponent is 3.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In 2 to the power 3, the base is 2 and the exponent is 3.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: 2 to the power 3 means 2 x 2 x 2 = 8.

topics.meaning: The laws of exponents are rules for multiplying, dividing and taking powers of numbers with exponents.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: There are simple rules, called the laws of exponents, that make working with exponents easier (for the same base). When multiplying powers of the same base, we add the exponents: a to the m times a to the n equals a to the (m + n). When dividing powers of the same base, we subtract the exponents: a to the m divided by a to the n equals a to the (m - n). When taking a power of a power, we multiply the exponents: (a to the m) to the n equals a to the (m x n). Also, any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1. These laws help simplify expressions with exponents quickly.
topics.easyExplanation: Laws of exponents (same base): multiplying adds exponents, dividing subtracts them, a power of a power multiplies them, and any number to the power 0 is 1.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Multiplying
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For the same base, add the exponents: a^m x a^n = a^(m+n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dividing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: For the same base, subtract the exponents: a^m / a^n = a^(m-n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power of a power
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Multiply the exponents: (a^m)^n = a^(m x n).

stepByStepExplanation.title: Power zero
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1.

examples: 2 to the power 3 times 2 to the power 2 = 2 to the power 5 = 32.
Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1, like 7 to the power 0 = 1.

topics.meaning: Standard form expresses very large numbers using powers of 10, making them easy to write and read.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Very large numbers are difficult to write and read in full, so we use the standard form (also called scientific notation). In standard form, a number is written as a number between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10. For example, 59,000 is written as 5.9 x 10 to the power 4, and the speed of light, 300,000,000 m/s, is written as 3 x 10 to the power 8. To write a number in standard form, we place the decimal point after the first digit and count how many places it moved to find the power of 10. Standard form is widely used in science to handle very large (and very small) numbers neatly.

topics.meaning: A figure has line symmetry if a line divides it into two identical mirror-image halves.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has line symmetry (or reflection symmetry) if there is a line that divides it into two parts that are mirror images of each other. This line is called the line of symmetry or axis of symmetry. If we fold the figure along this line, the two halves match exactly. A figure may have no line of symmetry, one, or many; for example, a rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry, an equilateral triangle has 3, a square has 4, and a circle has countless lines of symmetry. Many letters, shapes and objects in nature show line symmetry. Identifying lines of symmetry helps us understand balanced shapes.
topics.easyExplanation: A figure has line symmetry if a line splits it into two mirror-image halves that match when folded. A square has 4 lines of symmetry; a circle has countless.

topics.meaning: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being turned by less than a full turn about a centre.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if, when it is rotated (turned) about a fixed central point by some angle less than a full turn, it looks exactly the same as it did at the start. The fixed point is called the centre of rotation. The number of times the figure looks the same during one complete turn is called the order of rotational symmetry. For example, a square has rotational symmetry of order 4 because it looks the same after every quarter turn (90 degrees), and an equilateral triangle has order 3. Every figure looks the same after a full turn, but only figures with order 2 or more truly have rotational symmetry.
topics.easyExplanation: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a partial turn about its centre. A square matches 4 times in a full turn (order 4).

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A square has order 4; an equilateral triangle has order 3.

topics.meaning: Plane figures are two-dimensional (flat), while solid shapes are three-dimensional and occupy space.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Shapes are of two kinds. Plane figures are two-dimensional (2D); they have only length and breadth and lie flat, like squares, rectangles, triangles and circles. Solid shapes are three-dimensional (3D); they have length, breadth and height (or depth) and take up space, like cubes, cuboids, cylinders, cones, spheres and pyramids. Many everyday objects are solid shapes: a dice is a cube, a ball is a sphere, and a tin can is a cylinder. Understanding the difference between 2D and 3D shapes helps us describe and study the objects around us.

topics.meaning: Solid shapes are described by their faces (flat surfaces), edges (where faces meet) and vertices (corners).
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Every solid shape can be described by counting three things: its faces, edges and vertices. A face is a flat (or curved) surface of the solid, an edge is the line segment where two faces meet, and a vertex is a corner point where edges meet. For example, a cube has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices, and a cuboid has the same counts. A cylinder has 2 flat faces and 1 curved face, while a cone has 1 flat face, 1 curved face and 1 vertex. Counting faces, edges and vertices helps us identify and compare different solid shapes.

topics.meaning: A net is a flat shape that folds into a solid, and solids can be viewed from the top, front and side.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: We can understand solid shapes in two helpful ways. A net is a flat (2D) shape that can be folded to make a solid (3D) shape; for example, the net of a cube is made of six squares joined together, and a cylinder's net is two circles and a rectangle. We can also look at a solid from different directions: the top view, the front view and the side view each show a different flat shape of the same solid. These ideas help us connect 2D and 3D shapes, picture solids from a drawing, and are useful in design, model-making and reading plans.

importantKeywords.definition: A flat shape that folds into a solid.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A cube's net is six squares.

importantKeywords.definition: How a solid looks from a direction.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The top view of a cube is a square.

importantKeywords.definition: A 3D shape.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A cuboid is a solid shape.

topics.quickSummary: A net is a flat shape that folds into a solid (six squares into a cube), and solids can be seen from the top, front and side views, connecting 2D and 3D shapes.
revisionNotes: Net = flat shape that folds into a solid.
Solids have top, front and side views.
Nets and views connect 2D and 3D shapes.
